illnesses.name: Cholera
illnesses.description: A severe intestinal infection caused by contaminated water. Cholera was the single deadliest disease on the Oregon Trail, killing thousands of emigrants, sometimes within hours of the first symptoms.
illnesses.symptoms: Severe cramps, vomiting, and extreme thirst. The afflicted person grows very weak very quickly.
illnesses.death_message: {name} has died of cholera. The disease struck swiftly, and despite the family's best efforts, there was nothing more that could be done. May God grant {name} eternal rest.
illnesses.historical_note: Cholera epidemics swept along the trail in 1849-1852. One estimate suggests that up to 10,000 emigrants died of cholera during the peak years.

illnesses.name: Typhoid Fever
illnesses.description: A bacterial infection spread through contaminated food and water. Typhoid fever brought high temperatures and delirium, keeping emigrants bedridden for weeks at a time.
illnesses.symptoms: High fever, headache, stomach pain, and confusion. The sick person may become delirious and unable to recognize family members.
illnesses.death_message: {name} has succumbed to typhoid fever after days of burning with fever. The wagon train pauses to lay {name} to rest beside the trail. Eternal rest grant unto {name}, O Lord.

treatment_options.text: Rest, clean water, and cool cloths

illnesses.historical_note: Typhoid was common along the trail due to poor sanitation at camping grounds used by thousands of emigrants over the season.

illnesses.name: Dysentery
illnesses.description: A painful intestinal illness caused by bacteria in unclean water or food. Dysentery weakened emigrants terribly and made travel extremely difficult.
illnesses.symptoms: Severe stomach cramps, fever, and weakness. The sick person can barely walk or ride in the wagon.
illnesses.death_message: {name} has died of dysentery. Weakened beyond recovery, {name} passed quietly during the night. The family prays for God's mercy and {name}'s eternal peace.
illnesses.historical_note: Dysentery was so common on the trail that it was sometimes called 'the bloody flux.' It was a leading cause of death among emigrants.

illnesses.name: Measles
illnesses.description: A highly contagious disease that spread quickly through wagon trains. Measles was especially dangerous for children and could sweep through an entire company in days.
illnesses.symptoms: High fever, cough, and a spreading rash. The sick person's eyes become red and sensitive to light.
illnesses.death_message: {name} has died of measles. The fever was too strong, and despite constant care, {name} could not recover. Lord, welcome {name} into Your heavenly kingdom.
illnesses.historical_note: Measles outbreaks were particularly devastating among Native American communities along the trail, who had no previous exposure to the disease.

illnesses.name: Exhaustion
illnesses.description: The relentless physical demands of trail life — walking 15-20 miles a day, setting up camp, hauling water, and enduring harsh weather — sometimes pushed emigrants beyond their limits.
illnesses.symptoms: Extreme tiredness, difficulty walking, poor appetite, and trouble sleeping despite being worn out.
illnesses.death_message: {name} has collapsed from exhaustion and could not be revived. The long miles finally took their toll. May {name} now find the rest that the trail would not give. Requiescat in pace.

treatment_options.text: Take a full day of rest
Ride in the wagon and do light duties

illnesses.historical_note: Most emigrants walked the entire 2,000-mile journey. Only the sick, very young, and elderly rode in the wagons, which were too full of supplies for passengers.

illnesses.name: Broken Leg
illnesses.description: Accidents on the trail were common — a fall from a wagon, a kick from an ox, or a stumble on rocky terrain could result in a broken bone. With no doctors available, setting a broken leg was difficult and painful.
illnesses.symptoms: Intense pain, swelling, and inability to walk. The injured person must ride in the wagon, which is itself a painful experience on the rough trail.
illnesses.death_message: {name}'s broken leg became badly infected, and without proper medical care, the infection spread. {name} passed away despite the family's devoted care. God rest {name}'s soul.

treatment_options.text: Splint the leg and rest in the wagon
Splint the leg and use pain medicine

illnesses.historical_note: Accidental injuries were among the leading causes of death on the trail, second only to disease. Gunshot wounds and being run over by wagon wheels were particularly common.

illnesses.name: Snakebite
illnesses.description: Rattlesnakes were a constant danger on the plains and in rocky terrain. A venomous bite could be fatal without treatment, and even with care, the victim faced days of pain and swelling.
illnesses.symptoms: Sharp pain at the bite, rapid swelling, dizziness, and difficulty breathing. The bitten area turns dark and swollen.
illnesses.death_message: {name} was bitten by a rattlesnake and despite all efforts to draw out the venom, the poison proved too strong. {name} died peacefully with family nearby. Into Your hands, O Lord, we commend {name}'s spirit.
illnesses.historical_note: Rattlesnake bites were a real hazard on the Oregon Trail. Emigrants learned to check their boots and bedrolls carefully each morning.
